one_line_takeaway: The first large-scale PHP secure-coding audit of an LLM: across 2,500 zero-shot GPT-4-generated PHP sites, 11.16% were directly exploitable via Burp, 78% of file-upload sites lacked extension checks, and 54% of SQL-using sites omitted prepared statements — 2,440 confirmed vulnerable parameters in total.
key_finding: GPT-4-generated PHP is highly prone to vulnerabilities in a zero-shot setting. Burp active scanning flagged 11.16% of sites as directly exploitable, finding 901 vulnerable parameters (459 SQLi, 57 stored XSS, 394 reflected XSS). Static analysis found 78% (376/482) of file-upload sites lacked extension checks (CWE-434) and 54.28% of SQL-query sites lacked prepared statements (CWE-89). In total 2,440 vulnerable parameters were confirmed. On RQ2 (realism), manual review of 50 clean sites found 33 (66%) were too simplistic for real-life deployment or even for a vulnerability to be exploitable/detectable, revealing that longer multi-instruction prompts reduce site complexity.
core_contribution: The paper contributes ChatPHP-DB, a dataset of 2,500 standalone GPT-4-generated PHP websites (each an index.php paired with an init.sql database file), together with a per-sample vulnerability classification. The authors' own novel contributions are (1) the dataset itself and (2) the labelling of each program for four vulnerability classes (Insecure File Upload, SQL Injection, Stored XSS, Reflected XSS) via a combined active-scan, static-scan, and manual-verification pipeline. They frame this as the first large-scale study assessing the secure-coding capability of LLMs specifically for PHP. Note that the FormAI (112,000 C programs) and ReFormAI (60,000 SystemVerilog designs) datasets are cited as PRIOR work by others/related efforts, not as this paper's output; DiverseVul is likewise cited related work. This paper's dataset is PHP-specific and web-application-focused.
threat_model: The concern is that developers using LLMs (GPT-4 / Copilot X-style tools) for code generation will deploy insecure PHP without further review. The prompts are deliberately 'neutral' (zero-shot, no explicit request for secure code) to mimic real code-generation/completion usage where a developer does not ask for security. The adversary is an ordinary web attacker interacting with the deployed site via its web interface (SQLi, XSS injection, malicious file upload). The paper motivates the neutral-prompt choice by citing prior evidence that LLMs fail to fix vulnerabilities even when explicitly told to (Tihanyi et al.) and a type-juggling '==' vs '===' loose-comparison example from Nehorai.
limitations: The authors acknowledge: (1) they halted incremental testing after ~1,000 sites once the vulnerability distribution stabilized, and 2,500 small websites may not generalize (cost of GPT-4 API calls drove the design); (2) many overly-simplistic sites (static content, no user input/rendering) cannot exhibit or reveal vulnerabilities, deflating the detection ratio yet retained deliberately to answer the realism RQ2; (3) coverage is limited to four vulnerability types (SQLi, XSS-stored, XSS-reflected, file upload) out of OWASP Top 10 — Broken Access Control, Cryptographic Failures, etc. are excluded because they need contextual triggers absent in small sites; (4) static-analysis flags (e.g., missing prepared statements) do not always equal directly exploitable vulnerabilities. Only a single model (GPT-4 for PHP) is studied, so no cross-model secure-coding comparison is made.

datasets_used: ChatPHP-DB (the paper's own contribution): 2,500 GPT-4-generated standalone PHP websites, each an index.php paired with a GPT-3.5-generated init.sql; publicly released on GitHub (github.com/Beckyntosh/ChatPHP)
FormAI (prior/related work, NOT this paper's): 112,000 GPT-3.5-generated C programs, cited with 51.24% vulnerability incidence
DiverseVul (cited related work): existing non-AI-generated vulnerability-labelled codebase
quantitative_results: Dataset size: 2,500 PHP sites (index.php + init.sql pairs)
Burp active scan flagged 11.16% of sites as directly exploitable (abstract also states 11.56%)
Dynamic scan: 901 vulnerable parameters total = 459 SQL injection + 57 stored XSS + 394 reflected XSS
Unique sites per class (dynamic): SQLi 175, stored XSS 36, reflected XSS 202
File upload: 482 sites had upload functionality; 376 (approx. 78%) lacked extension checks (CWE-434)
Prepared statements: only 1,143 sites (45.72%) used them; 54.28% lacked them (CWE-89); among 2,293 SQL-query sites at least 50.15% lacked prepared statements
Adding static scan results, 26% of sites had at least one web-exploitable vulnerability (abstract)
Manual code audit (Table 1, 50 per class): SQLi 2 false-positive / 48 true-positive; XSS-Stored 0 FP / 50 TP; XSS-Reflected 0 FP / 50 TP; File Upload 0 FP / 50 TP

Penetration test of 50 'non-vulnerable' sites: found 4 additional exploitable (1 SQLi false-negative, 3 stored-XSS false-negatives); of 46 true negatives, 33 were too simple to exploit and 13 sufficiently complex
Random-50-site manual sample: 19 (38%) had vulnerable parameters
RQ2 realism: 33 of 50 clean sites (66%) were overly simplistic / not deployment-ready
Vulnerabilities span at least 6 MITRE CWEs: CWE-89, CWE-79, CWE-434, CWE-20, CWE-80, CWE-83
baselines_compared: No ML/detector baseline is compared; this is a single-model measurement study.
Generation models: GPT-4 (gpt-4-0125-preview) used for PHP code because GPT-3.5 could not consistently produce satisfactory PHP; GPT-3.5 used only for the cheaper init.sql generation.
Detection methods cross-validated against each other: Burp Suite Professional dynamic scanning vs. custom Python static analysis vs. manual code audit / white-box penetration testing.
Prior AI-generated vulnerability datasets (FormAI, ReFormAI) referenced as context/comparison points, not run as baselines.
